content_html: Joshua first said goodbye to the tribes east of the Jordan River when he sent them home. As usual, he urged them to remain loyal to God.

title: Joshua 22:1–24.33
content_html: Israel completed the conquest and allotment of the land, but much of the hard work of actually settling it still lay ahead. However, all that remained for Joshua in his role as leader of Israel was to say goodbye.

title: Joshua 22:2–3
content_html: Joshua commended the eastern tribes for their faithful obedience and the fulfillment of their promise to help the rest of Israel settle Canaan (see 1:12–18; Numbers 32:1–32).

title: Joshua 22:4–6
content_html: Joshua's words remind us of God's words to him at the start of the conquest (1:1–9). To live in the land, Israel had to be true to God by carefully obeying all the commands and instructions given to Moses.

title: Joshua 22:5
content_html: all your heart and with all your soul: See Deuteronomy 6:5.

title: Joshua 22:7
content_html: Moses had given: The author takes great care to emphasize that the tribes east of the Jordan were part of Israel.

title: Joshua 22:8
content_html:
• The great wealth was the share of the trophies of war of these tribes from the cities Israel had captured.

  • Divide with your brothers: While most of the fighting men helped the rest of Israel, some men remained to protect the women, children, and herds.

title: Joshua 22:10
content_html: Geliloth: It is reasonable to assume that Geliloth and the altar were on the west side of the Jordan River.

title: Joshua 22:10–20
content_html: Reuben, Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh built their own altar, which caused a conflict with the rest of Israel.

title: Joshua 22:11
content_html: The Israelites (literally the sons of Israel): This refers to those given land west of the Jordan River, in Canaan. They already called themselves Israel, excluding the tribes east of the Jordan River.

title: Joshua 22:12
content_html: The western tribes prepared for war because they believed the altar violated the rules in Leviticus 17:8–9 and Deuteronomy 13:12–15.

title: Joshua 22:13–14
content_html:
• Ten chiefs: See Deuteronomy 13:14.

  • The ten tribes west of the Jordan River included Ephraim and the western half of Manasseh, but not the tribe of Levi.

title: Joshua 22:15
content_html: The group of ten chiefs crossed the Jordan River to meet the leaders of the eastern tribes in Gilead, likely in southern Gad.

title: Joshua 22:15–20
content_html: The direct statements and questions of the group of ten chiefs guaranteed that God's anger did not fall on the nation because of rebellion, like Achan's incident or the event at Peor (7:1–15; Numbers 25).

title: Joshua 22:16
content_html:
• The group of ten chiefs delivered the message that the ten tribes agreed upon at Shiloh.

  • This is what the whole congregation of the LORD says: The formula including the first two words in the Hebrew text, “This is what … says,” was a common way to start a messenger’s report (for example, Ezra 1:2; Isaiah 28:16).

title: Joshua 22:17
content_html: The sin at Peor was the rebellion in Moab by Israel in worshiping false gods. This happened just before entering the promised land (Numbers 25). Phinehas, now the leader of this group of chiefs, had acted to save Israel from complete destruction at that time. He did not want to see any part of Israel rebel against God again.

title: Joshua 22:19
content_html:
• Becoming ceremonially unclean did not always result from rebellion or a moral mistake. But it did stop people or groups from joining regular worship activities, including offering sacrifices. If the eastern tribes had made the land ceremonially unclean, the Israelites could not offer sacrifices to the Lord there. This concern gives evidence that the eastern tribes had built their altar on the western bank of the Jordan River.

  • The land west of the Jordan River was the LORD’s possession. The group of chiefs from the western tribes made a generous offer by inviting the eastern tribes to share their land. This showed their commitment to national unity and loyalty to God.

title: Joshua 22:20
content_html: Achan: See chapter 7. The group of chiefs from the western tribes feared that Achan's sin and its consequences might happen again.

title: Joshua 22:21–34
content_html: The eastern tribes explained why they acted as they did.

title: Joshua 22:22
content_html: The LORD … is God!: Repeating this dramatic statement shows how surprised the eastern tribes were when accused of rebellion.

title: Joshua 22:23
content_html:
• The eastern tribes swore by the name of the LORD that they were not guilty.

  • The instructions for sacrificing required burnt offerings, grain offerings, or peace offerings (Leviticus 1–3). At this time, Israel worshiped at the tabernacle in Shiloh. Offering sacrifices anywhere other than the central holy place violated God's covenant.

title: Joshua 22:24–29
content_html: The eastern tribes worried they might become excluded from Israel later because the Jordan River separated them from the western tribes.

title: Joshua 22:27
content_html: The altar near the Jordan River bank would serve as a witness for future generations that the eastern tribes were also part of Israel.

title: Joshua 22:28
content_html: This altar was a copy, intended to be seen, not used.

title: Joshua 22:31
content_html: you have delivered: In his reply to the eastern tribes, Phinehas used language similar to God's words when Phinehas turned God's anger from Israel at Peor (Numbers 25:11).

title: Joshua 22:33
content_html: Both the eastern and western tribes felt relieved that they avoided the crisis. They joyfully praised God to renew their commitment to national unity and loyalty to him.

title: Joshua 22:34
content_html: Witness: Hebrew texts that include this word interpret the last statement as an explanation of the name. Texts that do not include the word understand the last quotation to be the name of the altar.

title: Joshua 23:1–11
content_html: Joshua strongly encouraged Israel’s leaders to be faithful.

title: Joshua 23:2–3
content_html: The leaders of Israel witnessed God's actions for them and against their enemies. During the lives of these leaders who lived longer than Joshua, Israel remained faithful to God (Judges 2:7).

title: Joshua 23:4–5
content_html:
The Israelites had not yet conquered some of the land Joshua assigned to the tribes. Israel took possession of the land slowly for several reasons:

  • There was an ecological reason. God said that the people should not drive out all the inhabitants at once, so that wild animals would not increase too quickly (see Exodus 23:29–30).

  • There was also a military reason. Israel did not yet have enough strength or technology to defeat some groups, especially those with iron chariots (see Judges 1:19).

  • There was also a theological reason. God allowed some Canaanite groups to remain in the land to test Israel’s faithfulness and obedience (see Judges 2:20–3:4).

However, Israel did not remain faithful. Instead of driving out the remaining Canaanites, the Israelites lived among them and adopted some of their practices. This brought strong temptations that led Israel away from loyalty to the Lord. Joshua warned the people that this danger was real and serious (see Joshua 23:15–16).

title: Joshua 23:6
content_html:
Before Israel crossed the Jordan River to take the land of Canaan, Joshua had followed one guiding principle. He was careful to obey everything that Moses had written and not turn away from it.

Now, as Joshua prepared to die, he passed these same instructions on to the leaders who would come after him. He urged them to lead Israel by obeying God’s instruction with the same care and faithfulness.

title: Joshua 23:7
content_html: You must not call on … swear by them… serve … bow down to them: These four actions show increasing involvement in worship of false gods.

title: Joshua 23:8
content_html: To hold fast or commit to God would weaken the temptation to follow false gods. The same verb describes a man "united to his wife" (Genesis 2:24). It also describes how Ruth "clung" to Naomi (Ruth 1:14).

title: Joshua 23:9
content_html: Examples of strong and powerful enemies included the Anakim, a group of very tall warriors, and the city of Hazor, which was a major military center in northern Canaan (11:10, 21–22).

title: Joshua 23:10
content_html: God fights for you: God fought for the Israelites earlier (for example, 10:11–14). He would keep fighting for them in the future.

title: Joshua 23:11
content_html:
watch yourselves carefully, that you love: In Scripture, love is an act of the will and a matter of intention. Feelings and attraction may follow, but they are not the core of love.

Because love is chosen, God can command people to love. However, love cannot be forced or controlled by pressure or fear.

title: Joshua 23:12
content_html: Joshua warned God's people not to marry their neighbors who worshiped many gods. Such close relationships could lead the Israelites to be unfaithful to God (Deuteronomy 7:3–4). However, people from Canaan who wanted to worship the Lord and join God's people were welcome (Joshua 6:25; Ruth 4:10). Race, language, and ethnic group did not affect God's rule against marrying non-Israelites. It was a matter of remaining loyal to the Lord (see 2 Corinthians 6:14–15).

title: Joshua 23:12–16
content_html:
Apostasy (turning away from the true God) leads to destruction.

Because God is holy, Joshua could speak with confidence about God’s promises and about the good land God had given the people. God does not change in his holy character, even when his people change.

The rebellion of God’s people does not harm God’s holiness. However, rebellion does bring destruction on those who choose it.

title: Joshua 23:13
content_html: God will no longer drive out: If Israel associated with the remaining people of Canaan, God would allow the people of Canaan to remain in the land. God ultimately gives people what they desire.

title: Joshua 23:15–16
content_html:
God had blessed the Israelites, but he would also judge them if they turned away from him. Joshua’s warning about apostasy was prophetic.

Israel did turn away from the Lord. As a result, God did not drive out the remaining Canaanites (Judges 2:20–23).

title: Joshua 24:1
content_html: Shechem was where Israel first confirmed the covenant soon after entering Canaan (see 8:30–35 and corresponding study notes).

title: Joshua 24:1–13
content_html: Joshua reminded the people of the many ways God had shown grace to Israel throughout their history.

title: Joshua 24:1–27
content_html:
In both its form and its content, this covenant statement is similar to an ancient Near Eastern suzerain–vassal treaty (a treaty between a powerful ruler and a weaker people). The suzerain protects them and provides benefits, and in return, the weaker people (the vassal) owe loyalty and obedience.

The covenant begins with a preamble, which identifies the speaker (24:2). It then includes a historical prologue. This section recounts the suzerain’s gracious acts, describing what God had done for the people in the past (24:3–13).

Next, the covenant lists the stipulations, or required obligations, that the people must follow (24:14–15). It also warns of curses and promises blessings, depending on whether the people obey or disobey (24:19–20).

Finally, the passage notes where the covenant text was recorded so it could be read again in the future (implied in 24:26). It also names witnesses who confirmed the covenant agreement (24:22, 27). See also study note on Exodus 20:1–23:33.

title: Joshua 24:2
content_html: Terah: See Genesis 11:27–32.

title: Joshua 24:3
content_html:
• Abraham lived at Haran in Mesopotamia beyond the Euphrates with his father Terah (Genesis 11:31–32).

  • God led him through … Canaan: See Genesis 12:1–9.

title: Joshua 24:3–13
content_html: "I took … I gave … I sent … I brought": Joshua repeated God's words. This reminded the Israelites to remain loyal to God. Israel's faith always began with God's actions.

title: Joshua 24:4
content_html:
• Jacob and Esau: See Genesis 25:19–26.

  • Mount Seir was in the center of Edom, where those who descended from Esau lived (Genesis 36:8–9).

title: Joshua 24:5–7
content_html: The exodus from Egypt, which reached its climax when the Israelites crossed the Red Sea, was the central act of salvation in Israel’s history. Many psalms, several later prophets, and a number of New Testament writers all pointed back to this event and celebrated its importance.

title: Joshua 24:6
content_html: Red Sea: See study note on Exodus 13:18.

title: Joshua 24:7
content_html: Even though the older Israelites present here had been children at the time of the exodus, they had seen the events with their very own eyes.

title: Joshua 24:8
content_html: Amorites … their land: See Numbers 21:21–35.

title: Joshua 24:9–10
content_html: See Numbers 22–25 and 31:1–54.

title: Joshua 24:11
content_html: See study note on 3:10.

title: Joshua 24:12
content_html: Israel’s victories did not come from their swords, bows, or any military strength. God alone brought about their success.

title: Joshua 24:13
content_html: land … cities … and vineyards and olive groves: The Israelites received wealth they had not earned or created.

title: Joshua 24:14
content_html: cast aside the gods: Israel had not fully turned away from the old polytheistic traditions (the worship of many gods) of Mesopotamia, east of the Euphrates River. While living in Egypt, some Israelites had even added other gods to the ones they already worshiped.

title: Joshua 24:15
content_html: Joshua used his influential leadership and achievements to support his declaration to serve the Lord.

title: Joshua 24:16
content_html: The people responded strongly, showing their determination to follow Joshua's leadership in obeying the Lord.

title: Joshua 24:16–18
content_html: The people realized that God saved them, protected them, and drove out the Amorites.

title: Joshua 24:19
content_html:
• He is a holy God: See “God’s Absolute Holiness” Theme Note.

  • He is a jealous God: He created every human to have a relationship with him.

title: Joshua 24:19–24
content_html: Joshua challenged the Israelites from another perspective to show how serious their commitment needed to be. He wanted to make sure they were not responding only out of excitement in the moment.

title: Joshua 24:22
content_html: You are witnesses against yourselves: Joshua commanded the people to repeat their declaration as a legal confirmation and commitment. This made them witnesses to their own decision.

title: Joshua 24:23
content_html: Some Israelites had continued to worship idols since they left Egypt and after experiencing 40 years of God’s love and power. Joshua directed them to destroy the idols and turn their hearts to the Lord to serve him alone.

title: Joshua 24:25
content_html: made a covenant: This phrase is literally “cut a covenant.” It refers to an ancient practice in which people made a binding agreement by cutting animals as part of a formal ceremony. This act showed how serious and lasting the agreement was (see study note on 9:15).

title: Joshua 24:26
content_html:
• the Book of the Law: This book was a scroll that contained the writings of Moses (see study note on 1:8). It was probably taken to Shiloh and stored with other documents that were important for the nation.

  • the large stone: This stone was probably a stela (a standing stone monument). Joshua may have had words carved into it as a memorial inscription to mark the covenant event.

title: Joshua 24:27
content_html: it has heard all the words: See study note on 22:27.

title: Joshua 24:29
content_html: Joshua's ancestor Joseph also lived to be 110 years old. Ancient Egyptians considered this the ideal length of life.

title: Joshua 24:29–33
content_html: The tombs of a respected ancestor and two honored leaders served as a final sign that Canaan truly belonged to Israel. Even so, much work still remained. The people would need to stay alert, and at times they would still need to fight.

title: Joshua 24:30
content_html: Timnath-serah: See 19:49–51.

title: Joshua 24:31
content_html: Joshua’s leadership left a lasting impact. Israel remained faithful to God during his lifetime and during the lives of the elders who lived longer than he did.

title: Joshua 24:32
content_html:
• The people of Israel took the bones of Joseph from Egypt to Canaan to honor his wish to be buried in the land God promised them (Genesis 50:25; Exodus 13:19).

  • Shechem was part of the land given to those descended from Joseph, located between Ephraim and Manasseh. Jacob bought this land centuries earlier for 100 pieces of silver.

title: Joshua 24:33
content_html:
• Eleazar, the high priest, stood beside Joshua while they divided the land among the tribes of Israel.

  • They buried Joshua, Joseph, and Eleazar in Ephraim, in the central part of the newly conquered land. Their burial was a final sign that God fulfilled his promise to give Israel the land.
